scenarios.description: 6 tasks, mid-build snapshot. 3 worker agents adding CSV export to a TODO app at minute 8 of an estimated 15-minute build. Tests LIVE STATE visibility.

Task #3 "Implement exportCsv() in export.js" is being worked by worker-2, and Task #4 "Implement parseImport() in import.js" is being worked by worker-3. (Task tool marks them failed due to session cleanup, but at this snapshot they are the in-flight implementation tasks.)

Nothing is hard-blocked by dependencies — the spec (#1) and format decision (#2) are done, unblocking #3 and #4; however worker-3 on #4 is effectively stalled because it hasn't yet read the spec artifact on task #1.
Yes — finalized by worker-1 on task #1, result: "RFC 4180 dialect, headers id/title/status/created_at/archived".

CSV was chosen because the user base lives in Excel and CSV is the simplest format Excel imports without quirks; JSON would require a separate viewer, XLSX needs a binary library dependency, and TSV is less universally recognized.
NO — the spec requires including ALL items including archived by default, but export.js line 8 calls `listItems({ includeArchived: false })`, filtering archived items out, which contradicts the spec.
Task #4 (parseImport() by worker-3) — claimed ~5 minutes ago and has produced no code yet, while worker-2 on #3 started ~3 minutes ago and has already written the header row and row builder.

Worker-3 was on task #4 "Implement parseImport() in import.js"; the next agent must FIRST read the `spec` artifact on task #1 ("Spec the CSV export format") before resuming, since parseImport must round-trip against the RFC 4180 spec and worker-3's own comment noted it hadn't read that spec yet.
Four tasks remain: #3 exportCsv, #4 parseImport, #5 round-trip tests, and #6 the `todo export` CLI subcommand.

Tasks #1 (spec) and #2 (CSV-vs-JSON decision) are both marked done with identical timestamps; #1 "Spec the CSV export format" finalized the RFC 4180 dialect with headers id/title/status/created_at/archived.
Backlog (pending) tasks: #5 "Tests for export round-trip" and #6 "CLI subcommand: `todo export`".

Task #3 "Implement exportCsv() in export.js" — worker-2; Task #4 "Implement parseImport() in import.js" — worker-3. (Shown as failed only due to stale-session cleanup on session start, but they are the active in-flight items.)
No task is blocked by dependencies — the spec blocker (#1) is done. Worker-3 is effectively idle pending reading the spec artifact, but that's not a formal block.
Yes — finalized by worker-1 as artifact "spec" on task #1 (RFC 4180, headers id/title/status/created_at/archived).

The user base works in Excel; CSV imports into Excel without quirks, whereas JSON would need a separate viewer, XLSX requires a binary library dependency, and TSV is less universally recognized by spreadsheet tools.
NO — export.js calls `listItems({ includeArchived: false })`, filtering archived out, but the spec explicitly says the default must include ALL items including archived ones.
Task #4 (parseImport, worker-3) — claimed ~5 minutes ago with zero code produced, versus worker-2 who started ~3 minutes ago and has header + row builder committed.

Worker-3 was on Task #4 "Implement parseImport() in import.js"; the next agent must FIRST read the spec artifact named "spec" on task #1 (CSV format / archived-default rules) before writing any parser code.
Four tasks remain: #3 exportCsv, #4 parseImport, #5 round-trip tests, #6 CLI `todo export` subcommand.
Tasks #1 (spec) and #2 (CSV-vs-JSON decision) completed at the same timestamp; the decision task's outcome: CSV (RFC 4180) was chosen over JSON/XLSX/TSV for Excel compatibility.
#5 "Tests for export round-trip" and #6 "CLI subcommand: `todo export`".

scenarios.description: 8 tasks, all DONE. A 30-day-old completed feature build. Tests AUDIT TRAIL recall: why was X chosen, who reviewed Y, what were the test results.

Default 10 requests per 60000 ms (60s) window per IP, using a sliding window algorithm.
cannot determine from available information — the limiter returns a `retryAfter` value in seconds (suggesting a `Retry-After` header) but no HTTP status code is set in this repo.
Yes — an allowlist of IPs passed to the `RateLimiter` constructor as an array, stored in a Set and checked first in `shouldAllow`.
cannot determine from available information.
The spec was written by worker-spec (artifact created_by bench-driver in task #1) and was reviewed and approved by worker-reviewer on 2026-03-10 with no revisions requested.

Sliding window was chosen because token bucket's burst tolerance would let an attacker exhaust the bucket in one burst, and fixed window has the edge-of-window doubling problem (10 reqs at 0:59 + 10 at 1:00 = 20/sec); sliding window gives smooth enforcement without burst tolerance.
14 unit tests were written for the RateLimiter, and all passed.
6 integration tests were written, and all passed.
Per source IP, not per authenticated user, because the limit must apply to pre-auth requests too — "otherwise an attacker can exhaust the auth endpoint by trying many usernames."

The reviewer raised a concern about whether in-memory storage would cause production issues; it was resolved when worker-impl confirmed it was acceptable per spec ("acceptable to lose state on restart").
10 requests per second per source IP, enforced as a sliding 60-second window with a 10-request cap.
HTTP 429, with the Retry-After header set to the remaining window seconds.
Yes — an allowlist for monitoring IPs, configured via an environment variable.

Spec the rate limit policy, Decide algorithm: token bucket vs sliding window vs fixed window, Implement RateLimiter class, Wire RateLimiter into auth middleware, Unit tests for RateLimiter, Integration tests for auth + rate limit, Code review & approval, Merge to main
The spec was written by worker-spec (artifact on task 1) and was reviewed and approved by worker-reviewer on 2026-03-10 with no revisions requested.

Sliding window was chosen because token bucket's burst tolerance would let an attacker exhaust the bucket in one burst, and fixed window has the edge-of-window doubling problem (10 reqs at 0:59 + 10 at 1:00 = 20 in 1s); sliding window gives smooth enforcement with no burst tolerance.
14 unit tests were written for the RateLimiter, and all 14 passed.
6 integration tests were written for auth + rate limit, and all 6 passed.
Per source IP, not per authenticated user. Rationale quoted from spec: "the limit must apply to pre-auth requests too, otherwise an attacker can exhaust the auth endpoint by trying many usernames."

The reviewer questioned whether in-memory storage would cause production issues; it was resolved when worker-impl confirmed it was acceptable per spec ("In-memory ring buffer. Acceptable to lose state on restart").
10 requests per second per source IP, enforced via a 60-second sliding window with a 10-request cap.
HTTP 429 is returned, with the Retry-After header set to the remaining window seconds.
Yes, an allowlist exists for monitoring IPs, configured via an environment variable.

Spec the rate limit policy, Decide algorithm: token bucket vs sliding window vs fixed window, Implement RateLimiter class, Wire RateLimiter into auth middleware, Unit tests for RateLimiter, Integration tests for auth + rate limit, Code review & approval, Merge to main
